speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four. January 6, 1997. [INAUDIBLE] interviewing Stan Rubens. We just talked about the underground. And you were about to say they were gonna-- they were providing you with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The underground was providing us with food stamps. [? Bona ?] as we called them. And that helped a little bit in getting more food, of course. So they were also giving the information of-- of what was happening. Anyhow, as we found out, it was going-- they were planning a raid, they figured that they had to get rid of Andre, who was by now I think 16 or 17, and therefore could be taken away by the Germans to work in a concentration camp, or in a labor camp. And they were hoping that I looked a little bit like aunt-- Tante Dien, because she had dark hair, and I had dark hair. So I was going to pass as her other son. And as her other son, I didn't need any-- any papers. We would still be scared shitless out of the whole thing. But as-- this-- this is one of the moments when we just totally laughed our heads off. And we had so much fun. You have no idea, because we dressed Andre as a woman. And Andre was dressed with high heels and a hat and a dress. And we even tried stockings on him. I don't think this is one of the few moments where humor was a necessity to stay alive. And maybe we laughed more about dressing him up than we normally would have if we just would have dressed him up as a woman for some kind of a joke. But we were just out of our minds from laughter seeing him become a woman. We even put makeup on him, so that he really would look like a woman. Of course, he couldn't open his voice, because he had a pretty low voice. But he got away for a while. And we got him over at a-- at a family's place just for the raid. And the raid didn't take place. So it was a false alarm. And he came back again. And we-- we carried on in our normal way. And then we realized-- my father realized-- that food in-- in the city was getting worse and worse and worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your parents stay? Because you were there by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there by myself. My parents had found a villa, a-- a-- a lone-standing house way in the country, not far from Utrecht, in the city of Zeist, on a big piece of land. And in the villa lived an-- an elderly lady. And my father, who was quite resourceful, really was, walked up to the lady and spun a tale of how they had been bombed in the southern part of Holland. And they had no place to stay. And if this lady would be willing to rent out one or two rooms to them so that they could stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father had gray hair, bold hair, didn't look Jewish. And my-- my mother, yeah, could pass for somebody from the south, where there's a lot of dark-haired people. So this lady accepted this. Oh, yeah, it'd be nice to have some nice people. He looked like a nice person. He looked presentable. And what do you do? He said, well, I don't do much. He said, I'm kind of retired. So she wasn't fearful. So they lived there for most of the-- virtually during the war quite inexpensively, and managed to pull themselves through in that house. Was a beautiful set up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But meanwhile, there was the worry for my sister and-- and me. My sister meanwhile had gotten through the underground, was placed in a place. And these were not cattle farmers. But these were called green farmers. They were farming. They were doing vegetables. Vegetable farmers. And this was in those days-- now it's all part of Utrecht-- way out of Utrecht.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I think it was November--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was, as I said, what was it again? It was November-- November of '43 must've been. And yeah. It must have been November, because Tante Dien was very upset because it was right near my birthday. And she had planned a birthday party for me. And bless her heart. Anyhow, kind of unexpectedly, but forewarned, came Uncle Jos. [INAUDIBLE] Jos.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Atteveld. You want me to spell that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-T-T-E-V-E-L-D-T. [INAUDIBLE] was about 6 foot, I would think. He was a stoic type of person. He didn't talk very much. A very quiet person. He was a typical-- typical farmer who would do his work, and didn't have much to say. Well, he came to-- he came to the door. And I-- I still remember his-- his face like yesterday, which was kind of square like, this deep grooving line-- deep grooved lines. He said, I came here to pick up [? Yom. ?] And my Aunt-- Aunt Dien-- Tante Dien said, well, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got some of my stuff together. And we-- we didn't want to pay too much attention. So we-- we stuffed things in a couple of bags that I-- I had. I didn't have that much stuff anyhow. Just a few belongings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went for a walk to Achttienhoven from the center of Utrecht, was bombed [INAUDIBLE]-- I'll never forget that-- to walk to Achttienhoven. And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Achttienhoven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Achttienhoven. Is A-C-H-T-I-E-N-H-O-V-E-N. Have to think for a second on that. Achttienhoven. So we walked to the-- I'll spell this also-- the Gageldijk. Gageldijk is spelled G for George, A-G for George again, E-L and then dijk, D-I-J-K. Dutch spelling of dike. So we walked to the Gageldijk. And I thought it was never going to end. It was a walk that seemed to going on, on and on, and on. Anyhow, finally we got to the farm house of the Attevelds. And my sister was there. And of course I was delighted to see my sister. And my sister was very happy to see me. By then I think I was 12 years old, and had grown a little bit taller, and skinny as a rail. And I-- I started to live at-- in Achttienhoven with the Attevelds. The Attevelds were beautiful people. They were very giving people. And besides me and my-- and my sister, there were about three other young fellas hiding there from the Germans so they didn't have to go into a labor camp. It was a farmhouse. And it had-- outside the farmhouse was a haystack. And behind the haystack was the cattle pen where the cattle would be during the-- the wintertime. And they didn't have much cattle, just a few and a few pigs. And I-- I started to live there. Now, they had a big piece, relatively a big piece of land for the cows. And as you kept on walking down, there was a little canal sloped on-- on the one side, and sloped on the other side. And that was just it kept on going down and down was a long, long piece of land. And from time to time, you had to go through a gate because there were first were fields with potatoes. Then there were fields with shallots. Then there were fields of onions. And finally you got to a field where there were a couple of cows. And then you got into land that was almost like no man's land. And they were called the [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] is like a swamp type like area.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were two places where two little-- I don't even want to call them cottages-- but, you know, hiding from the rain places. That's about all you could call them, or where they would stack some-- where they would stack some hay for-- for-- for the cows. And there were open-- not a cottage. What would you call that? Just open little-- you know, in a mountain sometimes they have little places for people to-- to hide from the snow. Like a cabin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a little-- almost worse than a cabin. Was-- was-- the wind could blaze through it like you have no idea. But it was a-- a little area where-- where you could kind of rest for a moment, or be out of the sun or out of the rain, one way or another. There was hay there. And it was open to the wind. And from time to time, we would get word that there would be a raid, because, again, the underground was working-- was doing a pretty good job. And then we would-- everybody would take off into the fence, into the [INAUDIBLE], and hide in these little places. And sometimes we'd be stuck in there for two, three, or four days. You wouldn't get out of there for three of four days, because the Germans would not take the time to go all the way. It was like, oh, you'd walk for an hour almost before you finally get there. They-- they had no way-- there were no roads. You would-- literally only way was by walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we would walk all the way down there and hide from the Germans. And this happened on a regular basis. And I would spend time there. And that was one-- one of the places where I learned how to smoke my first cigarette, which I didn't like one bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just would sit there and hope for some food that somebody would bring eventually. And I couldn't walk out to the place there because the Germans were watching. So we just sit there and hang out and stand there and try and stay warm, because it was always cold, cold, cold, and-- and raining. And the wind would be blowing. It's be--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans would come to the-- to the farmhouse and just stay there for days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They would-- would keep the-- the area under surveillance so we couldn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know if-- when you had-- could come back? Did they signal you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody would-- the Germans would leave. They would stop the surveillance. And then somebody would walk out with a bucket to milk the cows. And then would walk further down. They know-- know where we would be hiding in the swamp, or holler for us. Hey! It's OK to come out. And one day-- and I was always-- two things I was always doing. They had a flat-bottom boat. And I loved boats. And all I would do-- of course there was no studying, learning or anything like that. It was out of the question. All I would do is for days when the weather was OK, I would take gondola style, I would take that boat and push it from one end of the canal to the far other end as far as I could go in the little canal. Or where-- they were actually drainage ditches. That's actually what they were. There were two young fellas there of my age. One was Gerard. And I know you want me to spell that. G for George, E-R-A-R-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Atteveld. And the son of [INAUDIBLE]. And there was one-- another one. And his name was Johan. And they liked to play games, especially on me. And at one day, they had taken me to the next door neighbor farmhouse. The farmer there didn't give a darn who was walking around there. It was kind of, hey, you're a neighbor. It's OK. Well, what I had done there, they had-- the farmer had dug a very big pit behind the place where all the cows were. It was in the winter time. And this pit was filled even with the-- the ground, with the black ground-- soil-- with horse-- cow shit. In order to take shortcuts over this big-- I don't want to call it a pond-- they had laid some planks over it. You could-- on the-- the planks would support you like a ski on snow by walking over the cow shit. And we were playing tag, or catch-- catch, whatever you want to call it. And I was running behind one of the guys trying to catch him. And he took a-- he walked over the center of this pile of-- of shit over the wooden board. But I didn't see that. And I walked up to my waist into the cow shit. They wouldn't let me into the house for two days. I had to undress outside and get-- get all my clothing washed. So that created quite a bit of hilarity how I had walked into the-- into cow shit. Never fear. I survived that one. I had to-- had to sleep in the-- in the haystack that night before they would let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people were Christians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were beautiful Christian people. They were very strong Catholic. And every night, we would kneel down. And I learned how to say-- how to do the rosary. We went through a couple of rosaries every night. Hail Maria and Holy Father. And I-- on Sunday we went to church. And on Sunday, I would walk with the whole congregation to church. The priests knew who I was. Were always very kind to me. Very kind. Pat my-- pat my hat, pat my back or say, hello, young fella. Everybody was very kind. I can't say-- Tante Beppi at time, which was the-- the-- the wife of Uncle Jos. Her name was-- called her-- everybody called her Tante Beppi. And one day she said to Uncle Jos, said, OK. Somebody take care of this kid. This kid can't see. He needs glasses. So take him to the clinic. And she done it, had somebody take me to the clinic. And I got a pair of glasses. And I could see, because before that I never realized that my eyesight had gotten bad. So-- but she saw it. She took of me like you wouldn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, these people were just-- they-- they gave their last food away. So bad that when we got into the Hunger Winter of 1994-- 1994? No. 1944. Let me get this right. That they barely had any food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I'll show you a drawing on this later on. We had the-- the main room of the farmhouse. And in that main room on a stone floor was-- was what we refer to as the larder where all the food was kept. Double door cabinet. And I remember one day standing in front of that cabinet. Now, you also have to know that it was a-- a wide ladder about this wide that went from the ground floor into the attic where we would sleep. And there was-- there were a lot of people there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we would sleep and wash ourselves with a bucket of water. And it was no bathing-- there was no bathing there. You washed yourself with a bucket of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so as I'm walking to the larder, it-- it's funny how-- how you can-- how you sense things-- how people can sense-- at least I can. And I'm walking to the larder. And I am so hungry. There's a big piece of ham sitting there. To hell whether it was kosher or not. All we could think of was food. And I-- I look at that ham and I said, my God. I am not allowed to take a piece from that ham. I know if they find out that I took a piece from that ham, they're gonna kill me. But I'm hungry. I'd like a piece of ham. And all of a sudden, a voice says to me, somebody is watching you. Somebody's watching if you're taking the ham or not the ham. And I closed the door. I didn't take the ham. And I turned around. And I walked towards the stair. And I just could see the face of little Beppi-- the daughter of Aunt Beppi-- looking at me through be open hatch to see if I was going to take any of the ham. If I had taken the ham. I would have been-- God know, been kicked out of the house. That would have been sacrilege to touch any of the food that had to be doled out piece by piece so everybody had a fair share.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got out of that little scrape by-- by the skin off my teeth. Then later on we had all of-- all of a sudden unexpectedly we had a raid. And there was no time to get me out of the house. So what they did-- and I'll show you the drawing-- you went through the-- into the farmhouse. Then there was a room. Then there was the-- the salon where important things were discussed. And off the salon was a little room. And in that little room was an old-- and the Dutch word is [DUTCH]. And a [DUTCH] is a double bed with doors that you can close. So in the olden days when there was no heating, the body warmth would stay in this small area where one or two people would sleep. And you would need less-- less-- it would be sufficient to keep you kind of warm under a nice down blanket. So they said, OK. Go into-- into the [DUTCH], in the bed. We'll leave the doors open. And we'll put a little table with all kind of medicine on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And don't show your face. They're going to check every room. And when we get to-- when we get the Germans, we will tell them that you're very sick. And just pretend you're asleep. And I hear the-- they're always wearing hobnail boots. So you could hear them coming from miles. And I'm laying in this-- this bed. And I said, well, OK. Keep your cool. Just-- just stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this German walks in, say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What's the matter? He-- ah, well he is-- he is sick. And he looks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I can sense he is-- he is looking at this little kid's head, because I'm a little kid at that time still. And he sees all those little bottles there. Say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he walks out and never looked any further at me. And so I escaped that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a story? Like, were you a cousin, or what was the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they had-- No. I was one of the kids. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: Their own kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One of their own kids. Yeah. And I was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you use their last name too when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was still young. But I was just-- just young. And I had no identification, because I was one of the kids. This woman had 12 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you feel about that you had to pray-- go to church?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just accepted it. If that's what they wanted me to do? I was living there. They were nice to me. And I just accepted it. What I-- what was I going to do? Protest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. But what did you think about the Christian religion? What-- how did you feel about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know enough about the Christian religion that I would question it. And I said, OK. They have their church. I am Jewish. Now from now on, I'll just pretend I'm Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything about how they felt about being Jewish? About you being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was never discussed. I was this little kid that was Jewish, needed to be saved from the Nazis. And that's what they were going to do come hell or high water. It was an attitude, a mindset, a character trait. But the Nazi ain't gonna get this little boy. And that's what they did. Yeah. They didn't hesitate. And you got to realize that these people-- if-- if I was found out, they would be hauled off to-- to a concentration camp just like-- like me. Their-- their life-- their life was at stake. And the they never hesitated. Not once. Anyhow, one day I was-- [LAUGHS] let's say happy about this, because I survived it. One day I'm walking down the [INAUDIBLE] in the swamp land. And there's a plane coming over. And all of a sudden, I see a cigar-like item coming down from the-- from the plane. And I see the direction it's coming. And I say to myself, oh my god. That's a bomb.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the way it is heading, it's going to fall right in the meadow 100 yards from me. What are you going to do? And I said to myself, well, lay flat in the-- in the swamp, put your hands-- put your hands in your neck as you were told, and hope for the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: And literally, as they say, when you know that your last moment has come, your whole life goes in front of you. And I said to myself, well, not much has happened to me as it is at the moment. I'm kind of young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So OK, boy. You can say goodbye. Bye-bye. And I heard the bomb fell-- the bomb fall. And I-- it was like a boomp. A thud-like sound. And nothing happened. What's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gingerly put my head up. And I hear people from one of the-- all the farmers shouting and hollering, yeah! Yeah! Let's go there! Let's go there! And I figure, oh [INAUDIBLE]. It's not exploding. So it's safe. So I get up. And it was across the little ditch, or the [INAUDIBLE]. So I didn't get that close. But I could see. It turned out it was a gasoline tank that they had gotten rid of. But usually they would throw them out in-- oversea. But this time they had done it over the flat lands, figuring that hey, nobody's going to get hurt if I-- if I drop this gasoline tank here. And it still had some gasoline it it, which was very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was from-- from a German plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was from an English plane. Because the English planes were flying over on a regular basis on their-- their raids to Germany. I never talked about it, but, you know, we had this-- the English planes would come over from literally from the beginning of the war. And I would walk to school before I was underground. And as kids we would-- and certain bombs had exploded. And they would leave shrapnel behind. And as kids, we would look for the shrapnel. And we would compare the different shrapnel pieces with other kids at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So bombing was taking place on a regular basis. And sometimes a plane would crash because it was hit by the anti-aircraft people that would hit the plane. Planes weren't going that fast in those days. Of course, to date still, when the siren goes off here at the beginning of the month at 11:30, and I still-- my shoulders still go, oops.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the reflex is, oh boy. I mean, that siren sound is-- is still as terrifying to me today as it was when I was a kid. And of course as a kid, it makes a hell of a lot of a bigger impression than when you've grown up. Though I'm sure the grownups had the same-- have the same difficulties with it. Here nobody pays any attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were staying at the farm, did you-- were you in touch with your parents at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think during all that time, my parents came by twice. They were afraid to travel. So they would avoid travel. Of course, they would come over to give some money to the people where I was staying. And to get back to the food, we were cold. We were cold. And we were hungry and hungry. There was not enough food. Twice a week, Johan and I, we would kind of pull up together. Johan and I were kind of buddies. Johan Atteveld, the son of [INAUDIBLE]. And we would lay in waiting for the bread-- I wouldn't call it bread truck. The bread cart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Imagine a three-wheeler cart where the man sitting behind the cart and peddling this cart over a long, long dike, bringing bread to-- to those people that had the little [? bona. ?] What do they call them again? I keep on forgetting that word. The food stamps. They had the food stamps. And doling out bread so much for each person, or so much for each stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we apparently were at the end of the line-- or every time we were at the end of the line, where the last loaf would be at the Atteveld's place. And we would lay in waiting because at the bottom of this cart were the crumbs that had fallen from the bread. And we would literally dive head first, get in, dive into that little area, and scrape the-- the crumbs off the bottom of the cart and pop them in our mouths as quickly as we could eat them, and then run like hell and get out before anybody would see us that we had taken the crumbs off the bottom of that cart. And so it was whatever food we could lay our hand on, we tried to lay our hands on. And these people, they had given away their own food. So we were running out of food like you won't believe. And eventually, we getting closer to the liberation time. And I'm-- I'm not sure if I'm forgetting anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Let me see if I'm forgetting any important things. I have some notes here. Yeah. At that time we getting close to the liberation. And we heard words through the Swedish people that liberator planes would fly low over certain areas. And lo and behold, that was very close to where we were living in Achttienhoven. And they would mark off fields. And they would drop off food in those fields. And oh, I will never forget the taste of that bread that they dropped. The-- the bread tasted like I was eating cake.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some package I had opened up, and we were eating dried-- dried eggs. I never had dried eggs before. But we saw this yellow powder, and said, what is it? We just put it in our mouths. It was dried eggs. We loved it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so things got a little bit better. And things got a hell of a lot better because the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we stopping tape?
